title: Dog Scratching Ear Until It Bleeds: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog scratching their ear to the point of bleeding? Discover common causes, urgency levels, and how to help your pet get relief quickly.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your dog is scratching their ear until it bleeds, they are likely in significant pain from an infection, allergy, or injury and should see a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours.

What are the most common causes for a dog scratching their ear until it bleeds?

Aggressive scratching that leads to self-trauma is usually a sign of intense discomfort or pain, often caused by the following issues:

  • Yeast or bacterial ear infections
  • Ear mites or environmental allergies
  • Foreign objects, such as grass seeds, trapped inside the ear canal
  • Aural hematomas, which are painful blood-filled pockets in the ear flap caused by head shaking

Is it a medical emergency if my dog is scratching their ear to the point of bleeding?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While typically not life-threatening, it indicates significant physical distress. To manage this, you should:

  • Schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Seek care sooner if the ear is swelling rapidly.
  • Seek immediate care if your dog is acting lethargic or losing their balance.

How does sending a photo of my dog's ear help the veterinarian with triage?

Providing a clear photo allows the medical team to assess the severity of the issue before you arrive. This helps by:

  • Showing the extent of inflammation, swelling, or open wounds.
  • Identifying the color and consistency of any visible discharge.
  • Helping the clinic determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen.
  • Allowing the team to prepare necessary diagnostic tools in advance.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Excessive ear scratching in dogs, often leading to bleeding, can be attributed to pruritus secondary to conditions like atopic dermatitis or food allergies, where the ear canals are primary sites of allergic inflammation and secondary infection. This scratching can result in auricular hematoma development, characterized by fluid-filled swellings on the concave surface of the pinna. Treatment for hematomas involves surgical drainage, flushing, and placement of mattress sutures to eliminate the pocket; the addition of a drain can increase the success rate. In cases of chronic or severe otitis externa, or when neurologic signs are present, further diagnostics such as radiographs, CT, or MRI may be warranted to assess for otitis media or other underlying issues.

Chapter: Surgery, Emergency, General Principles

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2009)

faqs.question: Can I put anything in my dog's ear to stop the itching?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid putting over-the-counter drops or cleaners in the ear until a vet has checked the eardrum. If the eardrum is ruptured, certain liquids can cause permanent damage or deafness.

faqs.question: How can I stop my dog from scratching until my vet appointment?
faqs.answer: The most effective way to prevent further self-injury is to use an Elizabethan collar (the 'cone of shame'). This prevents the hind legs from reaching the ear and allows any existing wounds to begin healing.

faqs.question: Will an ear infection go away on its own?
faqs.answer: No, canine ear infections generally require medicated drops or systemic antibiotics prescribed by a veterinarian. Left untreated, they can spread to the middle or inner ear.
